metadata.category: Graph | DFS | DSU on Tree | Big Integer

content.problemStatement:
You are given a rooted tree consisting of NNN nodes numbered from 111 to NNN, where the ithi^{th}ith node has color CiC_iCi‚Äã.

The root of the tree is node 111.

Let FiF_iFi‚Äã denote the frequency of color iii in the tree, and,

SuS_uSu‚Äã denote the set of colors of all vertices in the subtree of node uuu.

For each node uuu (1‚â§u‚â§N)(1\le u \le N)(1‚â§u‚â§N), find ‚àèi‚ààS1‚àñSuFi\prod_{i \in S_1 \setminus S_u} F_i‚àèi‚ààS1‚Äã‚àñSu‚Äã‚ÄãFi‚Äã.

In other words, for each node uuu, find the product of frequency of colors that are present in the tree but **not** in the subtree of node uuu.

Since the product can be huge, print it modulo MMM.

Note:


The subtree of a node includes the node and all its descendants.
A‚àñBA\setminus BA‚àñB denotes the set difference of AAA and BBB.

Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test contains integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of nodes in the tree and mod.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers C1,C2,‚Ä¶,CNC_1,C_2,\ldots,C_NC1‚Äã,C2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,CN‚Äã representing the array CCC.
The next N‚àí1N-1N‚àí1 lines contain two space-separated integers uiu_iui‚Äã and viv_ivi‚Äã, denoting an edge between the nodes uiu_iui‚Äã and viv_ivi‚Äã.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers where the ithi^{th}ith integer should denote the answer for ithi^{th}ith node, modulo MMM.
constraints: 1 ‚â§ N ‚â§ 10^6 (sum of N over all test cases ‚â§ 10^6)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and M ‚Äî the number of nodes in the tree and the modulus.
- The second line contains N integers C1, C2, ‚Ä¶, CN where Ci is the color of node i.
- Each of the next N‚Äë1 lines contains two integers u and v denoting an undirected edge between nodes u and v. Node 1 is the root of the tree.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‚Äëseparated integers. The i‚Äëth integer is the product of the frequencies of all colors that appear in the whole tree but do NOT appear in the subtree of node i, taken modulo M.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: For the given tree F1=2,F2=0,F3=2,F4=1,F5=0F_1 = 2, F_2 = 0, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 1, F_5 = 0F1‚Äã=2,F2‚Äã=0,F3‚Äã=2,F4‚Äã=1,F5‚Äã=0. Also, S1={1,3,4}S_1 = \{1, 3, 4\}S1‚Äã={1,3,4}.

For node 111: S1={1,3,4}S_1 = \{1, 3, 4\}S1‚Äã={1,3,4}. Thus S1‚àñS1={}S_1\setminus S_1 = \{\}S1‚Äã‚àñS1‚Äã={}. The product of elements of empty set is 111.
For node 222: S2={1,3}S_2 = \{1, 3\}S2‚Äã={1,3}. Thus S1‚àñS2={4}S_1\setminus S_2 = \{4\}S1‚Äã‚àñS2‚Äã={4}. Thus, ‚àèi‚ààS1‚àñS2Fi=F4=1\prod_{i \in S_1 \setminus S_2} F_i = F_4 = 1‚àèi‚ààS1‚Äã‚àñS2‚Äã‚ÄãFi‚Äã=F4‚Äã=1.
For node 333: S3={1}S_3 = \{1 \}S3‚Äã={1}. Thus S1‚àñS3={3,4}S_1\setminus S_3 = \{3, 4\}S1‚Äã‚àñS3‚Äã={3,4}. Thus, ‚àèi‚ààS1‚àñS3Fi=F3‚ãÖF4=2‚ãÖ1=2\prod_{i \in S_1 \setminus S_3} F_i = F_3\cdot F_4 = 2\cdot 1 = 2‚àèi‚ààS1‚Äã‚àñS3‚Äã‚ÄãFi‚Äã=F3‚Äã‚ãÖF4‚Äã=2‚ãÖ1=2.
For node 444: S4={4}S_4 = \{4 \}S4‚Äã={4}. Thus S1‚àñS4={1,3}S_1\setminus S_4 = \{1, 3\}S1‚Äã‚àñS4‚Äã={1,3}. Thus, ‚àèi‚ààS1‚àñS4Fi=F1‚ãÖF3=2‚ãÖ2=4\prod_{i \in S_1 \setminus S_4} F_i = F_1\cdot F_3 = 2\cdot 2 = 4‚àèi‚ààS1‚Äã‚àñS4‚Äã‚ÄãFi‚Äã=F1‚Äã‚ãÖF3‚Äã=2‚ãÖ2=4.
For node 555: S5={3}S_5 = \{3 \}S5‚Äã={3}. Thus S1‚àñS5={1,4}S_1\setminus S_5 = \{1, 4\}S1‚Äã‚àñS5‚Äã={1,4}. Thus, ‚àèi‚ààS1‚àñS5Fi=F1‚ãÖF4=2‚ãÖ1=2\prod_{i \in S_1 \setminus S_5} F_i = F_1\cdot F_4 = 2\cdot 1 = 2‚àèi‚ààS1‚Äã‚àñS5‚Äã‚ÄãFi‚Äã=F1‚Äã‚ãÖF4‚Äã=2‚ãÖ1=2.

Test case 222: Since M=1M = 1M=1, all answers would be 000.

solution.approach:
For each test case we first compute the global frequency Fi of every color i and the product P = ‚àè_{i in S1} Fi (S1 = set of colors that appear at least once). The answer for a node u is P divided by the product of Fi for all colors that appear in the subtree of u. Because the division is exact (the denominator is a divisor of P), we can perform the division on an arbitrary‚Äëprecision integer and finally take the result modulo M.

To obtain the product of Fi for the colors inside each subtree we use the *small‚Äëto‚Äëlarge* (DSU on tree) technique:
1. Run a DFS from the root. For every node we keep a pointer to a hash‚Äëset containing the distinct colors that appear in its processed subtree and a big‚Äëinteger `prod` equal to the product of Fi for those colors.
2. While returning from a child, we merge the child‚Äôs set into the current node‚Äôs set. Always merge the smaller set into the larger one, which guarantees O(N log N) total insertions.
3. When a new color c is inserted into a set (i.e., it was not present before), we multiply the set‚Äôs `prod` by Fi[c].
4. After all children are merged, the current node‚Äôs set and `prod` represent exactly the colors present in its subtree, so the answer for this node is `ans[u] = (P / prod) % M`.
5. The root‚Äôs answer is always 1 because its subtree contains every color.

All arithmetic that involves the division is done with an arbitrary‚Äëprecision integer (Python `int`, C++ `boost::multiprecision::cpp_int`, Java `BigInteger`, JavaScript `BigInt`). The final modulo operation fits into 64‚Äëbit because M ‚â§ 10^9.

The algorithm runs in O(N log N) time per test case (dominated by the small‚Äëto‚Äëlarge merges) and uses O(N) additional memory for the sets and the tree representation.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (small‚Äëto‚Äëlarge merging).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the adjacency list, colour sets and auxiliary data.
